vulnerability.title: Record Events that Modify User/Group Information - /etc/security/opasswd
vulnerability.description: If the auditd daemon is configured to use the augenrules program to read audit rules during daemon startup (the default), add the following lines to a file with suffix .rules in the directory /etc/audit/rules.d: -w /etc/security/opasswd -p wa -k audit_rules_usergroup_modification If the auditd daemon is configured to use the auditctl utility to read audit rules during daemon startup, add the following lines to /etc/audit/audit.rules: -w /etc/security/opasswd -p wa -k audit_rules_usergroup_modification
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q audit && rpm --quiet -q kernel-core; then # Perform the remediation for both possible tools: 'auditctl' and 'augenrules' # Create a list of audit *.rules files that should be inspected for presence and correctness # of a particular audit rule. The scheme is as follows: # # ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- # Tool used to load audit rules | Rule already defined | Audit rules file to inspect | # ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- # auditctl | Doesn't matter | /etc/audit/audit.rules | # ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- # augenrules | Yes | /etc/audit/rules.

triage_decision.reason: Adding an audit rule to monitor changes to /etc/security/opasswd is a standard STIG hardening action that does not alter system functionality or security posture beyond enabling logging. It follows the policy for safe remediation of audit rules.

Let's produce improved plan.{"plan_description":"The remediation will create (if necessary) the audit rule file\n /etc/audit/rules.d/audit_rules_usergroup_modification_opasswd.rules and ensure it contains a single line\n\n-w /etc/security/opasswd -p wa -k audit_rules_usergroup_modification\n\nThe steps are:\n1. Ensure the directory /etc/audit/rules.d exists.\n2. Ensure the rule file exists (touch if missing).\n3. If the rule line is not already present in the file, append it.\n4. Set permissions to 600 and ownership to root:root.\n\nThis approach avoids duplicating the rule in /etc/audit/audit.rules, keeps the audit configuration minimal, and preserves the default augenrules loading mechanism. No restart of auditd is performed; the rule will be applied automatically on the next auditd restart or when augenrules loads new rules.\n"}

previous_review_verdicts.feedback: Review error, auto-approving: HTTPSConnectionPool(host='openrouter.ai', port=443): Read timed out. (read timeout=90)

The plan correctly adds the necessary audit rule to monitor /etc/security/opasswd for write and attribute changes, covering both augenrules and auditctl configurations, and therefore would resolve the vulnerability when executed.
concerns: The plan does not include steps to reload or restart the auditd daemon (or run augenrules) so the new rule may not take effect immediately.
It assumes the rule syntax will be acceptable as written; small formatting differences could cause a syntax error causing auditd to fail to load the rules.
The file existence logic could overwrite existing files if not careful – ensuring correct permissions and ownership is important.
If /etc/audit/rules.d is managed by a configuration tool, duplicating the rule in /etc/audit/audit.rules could create redundancies or conflicts.
suggested_improvements: After adding or editing the rule files, invoke `augenrules --load` or restart the auditd service to apply changes.
Use `lineinfile` or similar idempotent module to insert the rule, which automatically handles ordering, spacing, and validation.
Verify file permissions (600) and ownership (root:root) after creation.
Document the change and confirm auditd status with `auditctl -l | grep opasswd` to ensure the rule is active.
Consider removing the rule from /etc/audit/audit.rules if augenrules already provides it, to avoid duplication.
